topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee tasted like ash in my mouth. I stared at the printed email, the subject line a stark, unwelcome beacon: "Update on my career." It detailed a role at a prestigious design firm, a role my best friend, Liam, had assured me was a pipe dream, a fantasy he was chasing. My stomach tightened, a cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I replayed our last conversation—the carefully constructed narrative of Liam’s struggle, the practiced sighs of disappointment he’d offered. Now, the reality felt like a sudden, sharp drop. When I finally called him, the forced cheerfulness in my voice cracked. "So, the big break?" His voice, when it came, was smooth, practiced, utterly devoid of the vulnerability he’d shared with me just weeks ago. I just sat there, the phone heavy in my hand, watching the reflection of my own strained face in the dark screen.

[story 2]

I found the proof tucked inside an old shared cloud folder—a LinkedIn profile screenshot that didn't match the job description Liam had described. The air in my small apartment suddenly felt thick, suffocating. Every small sound, the hum of the refrigerator, seemed amplified. I traced the lines of the screen with a trembling finger, the digital evidence a tangible weight. A deep, hollow ache settled in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I thought about how easily I had dismissed his worries, how I had accepted his version of events without truly looking. When he finally walked in, whistling off-key, I couldn't meet his eyes. My gaze snagged on a chipped mug on the shelf, and I focused intently on the imperfection, wishing I could erase the last few hours, wishing I could rewind to before I’d trusted him so completely.

[story 3]

The truth landed in a quiet, devastating thud. I had spent the afternoon meticulously cross-referencing dates and company names, the silence of my living room amplifying the frantic hammering of my heart. It wasn't just a simple omission; it was a deliberate construction of a different life. A wave of profound disappointment washed over me, chilling my skin despite the warmth of the afternoon sun streaming through the window. I picked up my phone, my fingers stiff and clumsy. I wanted to apologize, to unravel the tangled deception, but the words caught in my throat, tasting metallic and inadequate. I watched the screen, the image of his fabricated success mocking my own naive acceptance. I felt a profound sense of failure, a heavy cloak settling around my shoulders, making every movement feel labored and slow.
